vocabulary.meaning: the way in which two or more people or things are connected
vocabulary.example: They have a very close working relationship.

vocabulary.meaning: a relationship in which a person or thing is linked with something else
vocabulary.example: The police are investigating his connection with the suspect.

vocabulary.meaning: responsibility for a mistake or misfortune
vocabulary.example: He took the blame for the team's failure.

vocabulary.meaning: responsibility for an accident or misfortune
vocabulary.example: It was my fault that we missed the train.

vocabulary.meaning: having lived or existed for a long time
vocabulary.example: He is an old friend of mine.

vocabulary.meaning: belonging to the very distant past and no longer in existence
vocabulary.example: They visited the ancient ruins of Rome.

vocabulary.meaning: a large number of people gathered together in a disorganized way
vocabulary.example: A large crowd gathered in the main square.

vocabulary.meaning: the assembled spectators or listeners at a public event
vocabulary.example: The audience applauded at the end of the show.

vocabulary.meaning: take delight or pleasure in an activity or occasion
vocabulary.example: I hope you enjoy your stay at the hotel.

vocabulary.meaning: make someone feel happy or satisfied
vocabulary.example: He did his best to please his parents.

vocabulary.meaning: give assistance to, especially financially or emotionally
vocabulary.example: My family always supports me in my decisions.

vocabulary.meaning: help someone, typically by doing a share of the work
vocabulary.example: The nurse assisted the doctor during the surgery.

vocabulary.meaning: having or showing a friendly, generous, and considerate nature
vocabulary.example: Thank you for your kind words.

vocabulary.meaning: having or showing behavior that is respectful and considerate of other people
vocabulary.example: It is polite to say thank you when someone helps you.

vocabulary.meaning: feeling, showing, or expressing sympathy
vocabulary.example: She was very sympathetic when I told her about my problems.

vocabulary.meaning: easy to like; pleasant and friendly
vocabulary.example: He is a very likeable young man.

vocabulary.meaning: easily agitated or alarmed; anxious or apprehensive
vocabulary.example: She felt nervous before her driving test.

vocabulary.meaning: easily annoyed or angered
vocabulary.example: He is always bad-tempered in the morning.

vocabulary.meaning: quick to detect or respond to slight changes, signals, or influences; easily hurt
vocabulary.example: She is very sensitive to criticism.

vocabulary.meaning: chosen in accordance with wisdom or prudence; practical and reasonable
vocabulary.example: It was very sensible of you to save your money.

vocabulary.meaning: the state of being with other people; association with another
vocabulary.example: I enjoy the company of my friends.

vocabulary.meaning: a number of people or things that are located, gathered, or classed together
vocabulary.example: A group of students stood outside the classroom.

vocabulary.meaning: liked, admired, or enjoyed by many people
vocabulary.example: Tennis is a very popular sport worldwide.

vocabulary.meaning: known about by many people
vocabulary.example: She became famous after winning the competition.

vocabulary.meaning: connected by shared feelings, interests, or loyalties
vocabulary.example: They are very close friends.

vocabulary.meaning: at or to a short distance away; close
vocabulary.example: The hotel is near the airport.

vocabulary.meaning: showing all the characteristics that you would usually expect from a person, thing, or group
vocabulary.example: It was a typical summer day, warm and sunny.

vocabulary.meaning: habitually or typically occurring or done; customary
vocabulary.example: We met at our usual place.

vocabulary.meaning: with no special or distinctive features; normal
vocabulary.example: It was just an ordinary weekend; we didn't do anything special.

vocabulary.meaning: not known or familiar
vocabulary.example: The author of the poem is unknown.

vocabulary.meaning: well known for some bad quality or deed
vocabulary.example: He is infamous for his dishonesty.

vocabulary.meaning: famous or well known, typically for some bad quality or deed
vocabulary.example: The area is notorious for its high crime rate.

vocabulary.meaning: calm and sensible; able to judge things well
vocabulary.example: She is very level-headed and handles stress well.

vocabulary.meaning: a large group of animals, or a disorganized crowd of people
vocabulary.example: We saw a large herd of elephants in the national park.

vocabulary.meaning: a person who uses strength or influence to harm or intimidate those who are weaker
vocabulary.example: The school has a strict policy against bullies.

phrasalVerbs.example: EX: She asked after my father and how he was doing.

phrasalVerbs.meaning: look after a child until they become an adult
phrasalVerbs.example: EX: She was brought up by her grandparents.

phrasalVerbs.meaning: fall in love with someone, or be tricked by a trick or joke
phrasalVerbs.example: EX: He fell for her immediately / I can't believe you fell for that silly joke!

phrasalVerbs.meaning: have an argument with someone and stop being friends
phrasalVerbs.example: EX: She fell out with her best friend over a minor issue.

phrasalVerbs.meaning: have a good relationship with someone
phrasalVerbs.example: EX: I get on very well with my colleagues.

phrasalVerbs.meaning: develop from being a child to being an adult
phrasalVerbs.example: EX: He grew up in a small town in the countryside.

phrasalVerbs.meaning: think that you are better than someone else
phrasalVerbs.example: EX: He looks down on anyone who doesn't have a university degree.
EX: Many young players look up to the team captain.

phrasalVerbs.meaning: become friends again after an argument
phrasalVerbs.example: EX: They argue a lot, but they always make up quickly.
EX: Her grandfather passed away last week.

phrasalVerbs.meaning: keep treating someone badly or unfairly
phrasalVerbs.example: EX: The older boys used to pick on him at school.

phrasalVerbs.meaning: criticize someone in a way that makes them feel stupid or embarrassed
phrasalVerbs.example: EX: He is always putting his wife down in front of other people.

phrasalVerbs.meaning: start living a quiet life in one place, especially when you get married
phrasalVerbs.example: EX: They decided to settle down and start a family.

phrasalVerbs.meaning: defend someone or something that is being criticized or attacked
phrasalVerbs.example: EX: You must stand up for your rights.

phrasalVerbs.meaning: surprise or shock someone very much
phrasalVerbs.example: EX: We were taken aback by his sudden announcement.

collocations.meaning: express support or agreement
be accepted or approved by someone

collocations.collocation: have an argument with sb about sth/doing
collocations.meaning: quarrel or disagree angrily with someone
succeed or fail in a verbal dispute
look after or be responsible for someone or something
look after someone, or feel concern about something
be brave enough to perform an action
require bravery to perform an action
wearing clothes that hide your true identity
put on clothes or a mask to hide identity
change your appearance so people do not recognize you
appearing as something or someone else
experience a series of thoughts or images during sleep or have an ambition
have pleasant thoughts that make you forget about the present
think of something you would like to happen
have children
a family consisting of two parents and their children
a family that extends beyond the nuclear family, including grandparents, aunts, uncles, etc.
perform a helpful service for someone or owe them one
support or agree with a proposal or idea

collocations.collocation: make / become / stay / be friends with
collocations.meaning: establish or maintain a friendship with someone
feel deep affection or romantic attraction for someone
feeling cheerful or angry/irritable
feeling suitable or unsuitable for an activity
wanting to do or have something
feel sorrow or sympathy for someone
show compassion or help someone who is in a bad situation
experience sympathy for someone's misfortune
it is regrettable or disappointing that
make a commitment to perform an action
commit yourself to do something for someone
fail to do what you committed to do

wordPatterns.meaning: have the same opinion or consent to something
wordPatterns.example: EX: I agree with your plan.

wordPatterns.meaning: have the same opinion as someone else
wordPatterns.example: EX: I agree with you on this matter.

wordPatterns.meaning: consent to perform an action
wordPatterns.example: EX: He agreed to help us.

wordPatterns.meaning: consent or concur that a statement is true
wordPatterns.example: EX: We all agreed that the price was too high.

wordPatterns.meaning: permit someone to perform an action
wordPatterns.example: EX: My parents allowed me to go to the party.

wordPatterns.meaning: permit something to happen
wordPatterns.example: EX: We do not allow smoking here.

wordPatterns.meaning: agree with or think well of someone or something
wordPatterns.example: EX: My parents don't approve of my new friends.

wordPatterns.meaning: give official agreement or permission to something
wordPatterns.example: EX: The headteacher approved the new school project.

wordPatterns.meaning: try to hurt or damage something using violence
wordPatterns.example: EX: The enemy attacked the city.

wordPatterns.meaning: criticize someone strongly for something
wordPatterns.example: EX: The minister was attacked for his controversial remarks.

wordPatterns.meaning: an act of violence or strong criticism against someone or something
wordPatterns.example: EX: The article was a personal attack on the prime minister.

wordPatterns.meaning: officially forbid someone from doing or entering something
wordPatterns.example: EX: He was banned from driving for a year.

wordPatterns.meaning: make someone believe that something is true
wordPatterns.example: EX: I managed to convince him of my innocence.

wordPatterns.meaning: persuade someone to perform an action
wordPatterns.example: EX: I convinced her to come with us.

wordPatterns.meaning: make someone believe that a statement is true
wordPatterns.example: EX: I convinced him that we were safe.

wordPatterns.meaning: make someone perform an action against their will
wordPatterns.example: EX: The robber forced him to hand over the money.

wordPatterns.meaning: compel someone to participate in an action or situation
wordPatterns.example: EX: She was forced into resigning from her job.

wordPatterns.meaning: not controlled or influenced by someone or something else
wordPatterns.example: EX: The country became independent of colonial rule.

wordPatterns.meaning: express opposition to or disapproval of something
wordPatterns.example: EX: I object to being treated like a child.

wordPatterns.meaning: behave as if you are someone or something else
wordPatterns.example: EX: He pretended to be asleep when I entered.

wordPatterns.meaning: behave as if you are performing an action when you are not
wordPatterns.example: EX: She pretended to read, but she was looking at him.

wordPatterns.meaning: behave as if a statement is true when it is not
wordPatterns.example: EX: Let's pretend that we are on a desert island.

wordPatterns.meaning: depend on someone or something with full trust
wordPatterns.example: EX: You can always rely on me for help.

wordFormation.meaning: able: having the power or skill to do something / unable: not having the power or skill to do something / inability: state of being unable to do something / disability: a physical or mental condition that limits a person's movements, senses, or activities / enable: make something possible / disabled: having a physical or mental condition that limits movements or activities
wordFormation.example:
EX: He is able to speak French.
EX: She was unable to attend the meeting.
EX: His inability to make decisions is a problem.
EX: He overcomes his disability with courage.
EX: This software enables you to edit photos.
EX: There are special parking spaces for disabled people.

wordFormation.meaning: achieve: successfully bring about or reach by effort / achievement: a thing done successfully, typically by effort, courage, or skill
wordFormation.example:
EX: She achieved her goals.
EX: Winning the award was a great achievement.

wordFormation.meaning: argue: exchange or express diverging or opposite views / argument: an exchange of diverging or opposite views / argumentative: given to expressing diverging or opposite views
wordFormation.example:
EX: They argue about money.
EX: They had a heated argument.
EX: He is very argumentative.

wordFormation.meaning: care: feel concern or interest / careful: making sure of avoiding danger or mistake / careless: not giving sufficient attention or thought / caring: displaying kindness and concern / uncaring: not displaying kindness or concern
wordFormation.example:
EX: He cares for his elderly parents.
EX: Be careful when you cross the street.
EX: It was a careless mistake.
EX: She is a very caring nurse.
EX: He was criticized for his uncaring attitude.

wordFormation.meaning: correspond: write letters to one another, or be analogous or equivalent / correspondence: communication by exchanging letters, or agreement
wordFormation.example:
EX: They corresponded for years.
EX: The police read their correspondence.

wordFormation.meaning: friend: a person whom one knows and with whom one has a bond of mutual affection / friendship: a relationship between friends / friendly: kind and pleasant / unfriendly: not friendly
wordFormation.example:
EX: She is a close friend of mine.
EX: Their friendship lasted a lifetime.
EX: The villagers were very friendly.
EX: Why are you being so unfriendly?

wordFormation.meaning: happy: feeling or showing pleasure or contentment / unhappy: not happy / happiness: the state of being happy / unhappiness: the state of not being happy
wordFormation.example:
EX: She is happy with her job.
EX: He was unhappy about the decision.
EX: Money cannot buy happiness.
EX: Her eyes showed her unhappiness.

wordFormation.meaning: jealous: feeling or showing envy of someone or their achievements / jealousy: the state or feeling of being jealous
wordFormation.example:
EX: She is jealous of her sister's success.
EX: His outburst was triggered by jealousy.

wordFormation.meaning: kind: having a friendly or generous nature / unkind: not kind / kindness: the quality of being friendly, generous, and considerate / unkindness: the quality of being unkind
wordFormation.example:
EX: Thank you for your kind offer.
EX: It was unkind of you to say that.
EX: She treated everyone with kindness.
EX: I was shocked by his unkindness.

wordFormation.meaning: marry: join in marriage / marriage: the legally recognized union of two people / married: having a husband or wife / unmarried: not married
wordFormation.example:
EX: They decided to marry next month.
EX: Their marriage lasted for fifty years.
EX: She is married to a doctor.
EX: He remained unmarried all his life.

wordFormation.meaning: nerve: a fiber that transmits impulses of sensation / nervousness: the state of being anxious / nervous: easily agitated or anxious / nervously: in an anxious or apprehensive manner
wordFormation.example:
EX: He has got a lot of nerve to speak to me like that!
EX: She tried to hide her nervousness.
EX: I feel nervous about the exam.
EX: She waited nervously for the results.

wordFormation.meaning: obey: submit to the authority of or comply with / disobey: fail to obey / disobedience: failure or refusal to obey / obedient: complying or willing to comply with orders / disobedient: refusing to obey rules or authority
wordFormation.example:
EX: You must obey the school rules.
EX: He was punished for disobeying his teacher.
EX: Her disobedience caused a lot of trouble.
EX: She is an obedient child.
EX: The disobedient dog refused to sit.

wordFormation.meaning: person: a human being regarded as an individual / personality: the combination of characteristics that form an individual's character / personal: belonging to or affecting a particular person / impersonal: not influenced by showing personal feelings
wordFormation.example:
EX: She is a very nice person.
EX: He has a strong personality.
EX: That is my personal opinion.
EX: The bank felt cold and impersonal.

wordFormation.meaning: polite: showing behavior that is respectful / impolite: not polite / politeness: the quality of being polite / impolitely: in an impolite manner
wordFormation.example:
EX: He was polite to his guests.
EX: It is impolite to point at people.
EX: We appreciate your politeness.
EX: He behaved impolitely towards the waiter.

wordFormation.meaning: relate: make or show a connection between / relation: the way in which two or more concepts or people are connected / relative: considered in relation or in proportion to something else / relationship: the way in which two or more concepts or people are connected
wordFormation.example:
EX: The film relates a story of adventure.
EX: The relations between the two countries have improved.
EX: Everything is relative.
EX: They are in a stable relationship.

wordFormation.meaning: willing: ready, eager, or prepared to do something / unwilling: not ready or eager to do something / willingness: the quality of being prepared to do something / unwillingness: the quality of being unwilling / willingly: of one's own free will / unwillingly: against one's wishes
wordFormation.example:
EX: I am willing to help you.
EX: He was unwilling to change his mind.
EX: She showed a willingness to learn.
EX: I was surprised by his unwillingness to help.
EX: He willingly accepted the offer.
EX: She went to the party unwillingly.
